original_text:
For far too long, I have tried to inculcate good habits. I'd promise myself that I'd change my life for the better and always failed to deliver on it. And then I'd completely forget about wanting to be better until I let things get so bad that I'd again think to myself, 'I need to change my life'. This loop went on for about a year.

However, these days, I have this burning desire almost every day. I still fumble, I still fail. But the intention is ingrained, which wasn't the case earlier.

I really hope I come through for myself and get better, a miniscule amount, every single day.
text:

These days, though, I feel a strong drive almost every day. I still make mistakes and still fail, but the intention is now consistent, which wasn’t true before.

I really hope I can show up for myself and improve, even just a little, every single day.

original_text:
Feeling a bit euphoric today - there's absolutely nothing more motivating than tacking a really hideous problem that's been weighing you down and getting past the emotional part of it.

My problem's still there, there's still work to be done on it - but I finally quit ignoring it, got all the information together to address it, enlisted some outside help, and took the first steps to solving it and have a plan to move forward (instead of ignoring it, berating myself for ignoring it, attempting it then stopping because I was terrified of the possible outcomes).

I had no idea how much dread this problem had created in my life - that constant nagging thought that it was out there and I needed to deal with it.  But the difference today vs two days ago is immense and I feel ready to take on the world.

This is really what my 2021 is all about - taking on all the "stuff" that's built up over the years that I've been putting off, ignoring, or avoiding dealing with - and I'm really excited to have some concrete progress completed and to feel this energy to take on some more. 

For anyone else out there facing something similar - you can do this.  Dealing with it will not cost you nearly as much anguish or energy as constantly running from it.

original_text:
So, today I talked to an ex friend that was close to me. And I sort of.. presented myself as fucking desperate and pathetic... like what the fuck was wrong with me?

We had a fight a few months ago and stopped talking. I was plagued by the thought of chasing the happiness that I had when I was with her. She goes to my school and we haven't talked in a long time... I just wanted to check if she was ok and maybe get some semblance of what was I guess.. recovering the "relationship".

 She was incredibly cold towards my presence and I should've stopped there but my dumbass kept going. After a minute of talking I just left to carry onto my final lesson whilst questioning my stupid actions that made me look pathetic, weird and desperate. I just... want to do my work, read and sleep. I just want that event to not exist. I just want her presence removed from my mind. I want that "need" to recover the happiness I felt to go. I'm such an idiot. I'm afraid to go back to school because we were surrounded by other students. I'm afraid they heard what happened. Should I just pretend that event never happened?
text:
Today I spoke to an ex-friend who used to be very close to me, and I feel like I came across as desperate and pathetic… I keep thinking, what was wrong with me?

We had a falling out a few months ago and stopped talking. I’ve been stuck on the idea of trying to get back the happiness I felt when we were friends. She goes to my school, and after not speaking for a long time, I just wanted to check if she was okay and maybe rebuild some version of our connection.

She was very cold toward me, and I should have stopped there, but I kept pushing the conversation. After a minute, I left and went to my next class, all the while overthinking how stupid I must have looked.

Now I just want to focus on my work, read, and sleep. I wish this whole situation didn’t exist. I want to stop thinking about her and stop feeling this need to go back to how things were. I feel like I messed up badly, and I’m even nervous about going back to school because other students were around. I’m worried people heard it all.

Should I just try to act like it never happened?

original_text:
I was planning a surprise bachelor party for my best friend and we had rented a van. 

Long story short I wasn't strapped in yet and the back doors weren't shut as the driver gassed it hard, as I flew out the back and hit the back of my head on pavement.

I don't remember any of this, and the doctor told me I had my first concussion - surely it could have been worse.

I've always lived life feeling invincible since I am young and healthy (25). But, this experience humbled me.

It told me life can change in an instant and it's so important to make the most of your life, since the present is all we have. It makes me want to act, take that trip I've always wanted, ask out that person I've always found attractive...

It made me put life in perspective.
text:
I was organizing a surprise bachelor party for my best friend, and we had hired a van.  

In short, I hadn’t buckled up yet and the rear doors weren’t fully closed when the driver suddenly accelerated hard. I was thrown out the back and hit my head on the pavement.  

I don’t remember any of it, and the doctor later told me I’d suffered my first concussion—though it could have been much worse.  

I’ve always felt invincible because I’m young and healthy (25), but this experience really humbled me.  

It showed me how quickly life can change and how important it is to make the most of the present, since it’s all we truly have. It pushed me to take action—go on that trip I’ve always wanted, ask out someone I’ve long been attracted to…  

It really put things into perspective for me.

text:

I know one day, if I keep this up, I would be able to do all those outdoor things without feeling them like a chore but actually enjoy them.

I'm too concerned about other people's lives, and not concerned enough about my own. As well, I used them as almost a digital scrapbook, to keep memories to look back on, but instead of just being about keeping memories, I ended up spending too much time looking backwards, and not enough looking forwards. So I saved and ordered physical prints of the most important pictures and memories, and permanently deleted everything else. No deactivating, just deleting.  Cold turkey.

The above combined with my compulsion to check on my ex-boyfriend's social media (and hurt my own feelings by doing so), pushed me to finally delete them all for my own mental health. It doesn't matter what he's doing. It doesn't matter what anyone else is doing. And it certainly doesn't matter what they think about what I'm doing. I need to learn to worry about and work on my relationship with myself before anyone else.

As a 22 year old female, I have a feeling this is going to get some funny reactions from my peers, especially new people I meet, but I think it's the best choice for me. It did nothing but make me feel inferior and insecure.

original_text:
I'm a truth warrior and I'm tired of all the lies. Obviously the internet is so full of lies that I would never sleep and die an unhappy death if I tried to combat the blatant dishonesty that abounds, but in the real world I feel like I'm on a mission.

I feel like I don't care if it offends someone. If someone or an organization is pulling some BS I'm going to speak up and tell them why I think it's BS. For instance, I emailed an organization that I felt was gouging their customer base in violation of the spirit of the service they offer. I was met with denial and not nice words.

I don't think this is good for me or anyone though. Then again, I used to be a doormat and let anyone and anything trample over me, so I feel like I'm leaning towards the other end of the scale again.

Does anyone have any suggestions on how to moderate this type of urge?
text:
I see myself as someone who values truth, and I’m getting tired of all the dishonesty around me. I know the internet is full of misinformation, and I could never realistically fight all of it, but in real life I feel like I’m on a kind of mission.

I’ve reached a point where I don’t really care if I offend people anymore. If I think a person or organization is doing something dishonest or unfair, I feel compelled to speak up and explain why I think it’s wrong. For example, I once emailed an organization I believed was overcharging customers in a way that went against the spirit of their service. They denied it and responded pretty harshly.

At the same time, I don’t think this approach is healthy for me or for others. But I also used to be a complete pushover and let people walk all over me, so I feel like I might be swinging too far in the opposite direction.

Does anyone have advice on how to balance or control this kind of urge?

original_text:
Hi all-- just needed to post this somewhere. Long story short, I have a super sensitive nervous system and dental work (especially on my molars) can be v painful no matter how many numbing injections I receive.

Over the last 6 months I've gotten several root canals, two of which failed because my dentist didn't know what she was doing (she was v nice, though). So this means I have to get those failed root canals DONE AGAIN, and this time each root canal will be in two phases (so basically a "double" root canal for each tooth). And I have more to go.

My depression and fear kept me from the dentist for too long, and I've decided that even though this SUCKS I'm going to stick with it because it's the best thing for me. Fear of dentists is strong in my family, so much so that my brother hasn't been in decades and literally keeps amoxicillin in his house for whenever he feels tooth pain.

So that's it. I'm doing the scary things. Going to get another root canal re-done today, and definitely thankful for any support.
text:
Hi everyone—just needed to get this out somewhere. Long story short, I have a very sensitive nervous system, and dental procedures (especially on molars) can be extremely painful for me even with numbing injections.

Over the past six months, I’ve had several root canals done, and two of them failed because my dentist at the time didn’t really know what she was doing (she was very kind, though). That means I now have to redo those root canals, and this time each one will be done in two stages—basically a double root canal for each tooth. I still have more dental work ahead as well.

My depression and fear kept me away from the dentist for too long, but I’ve decided that even though this is really difficult, I’m going to continue because it’s the best thing for me. Dental anxiety runs strongly in my family—my brother hasn’t seen a dentist in decades and even keeps amoxicillin at home in case of tooth pain.

So that’s where I’m at. I’m pushing through the scary stuff and going for another root canal redo today. I really appreciate any support.

original_text:
When you look back to everything that happened, you realize, Dr. Seuss was right, those were happy times, special moments of life, and yet why do we feel sad. 

The essence of this advice sparks a lot of sense. 

First, you should be happy that something happened (assuming that it is good and beneficial in a way).
 
Then, you should not feel sad because you'll always have that memory in you.

But maybe these reasons are why you are feeling down. Despite having them, you lose them, you built yourself around what you have, you adapted your living, therefore it becomes hard to know that it is over.

However, almost everything may be compared to idea, that is, nothing stays the same, and perhaps the life that we have. Moreover the idea of the quote feels so true, that we must be happy for an occurrence, or shall we wish it never happened.

They might be stages, when we are forced to recover for there is no other option to be better.

Note: The contents of this article is more directed to positive things, like achievements, friendships, and other similar experiences, although some negative occurrences may also be considered, such as break ups, accident, etc.
text:
When you reflect on everything that has happened, you start to realize that Dr. Seuss was right—those were happy, meaningful moments in life. And yet, it can still feel sad looking back. Why is that?

The core of this idea makes a lot of sense.

First, you should be glad that those experiences happened (assuming they were positive and beneficial).

Then, there’s no reason to feel sad, because those memories will always stay with you.

But maybe that’s exactly why sadness appears. Even if you had those moments, you eventually lose them. You build your life around what you have, you adapt to it, and so when it ends, it becomes difficult to accept.

In a way, almost everything can be understood through the idea that nothing stays the same—including life itself. That’s why the quote feels so true: we should be grateful something happened, rather than wishing it never did.

There may also be stages where we simply have to recover, because there is no other option but to move forward.

Note: This reflection mainly refers to positive experiences like achievements, friendships, and similar moments, though it can also apply to negative events such as breakups or accidents.

Ultimately, it ties back to the idea that nothing stays the same, including life itself. That’s why the quote resonates so strongly: we should appreciate that something happened, rather than wish it had never happened at all.

There may also be periods where recovery is necessary, since moving forward is the only option.

Note: This applies mainly to positive experiences like achievements and friendships, though it can also include difficult events such as breakups or accidents.

original_text:
I'm a long-term survivor with multiple self-harm in the past & a few failed suicide attempts. For once, I decided not to give depression a label to me. I want to be in control of myself, not that stupid D-word. I want to believe I can be in control.

Recently, I met someone new after being dumped last year. To be honest, it was at the most unexpected time. I wasn't searching, I wasn't even desperate to be with someone - it just felt natural, like everything fit into its place.

I told her all about my fears, my pain, every valid feelings I'm suffering from & for the first time in my life, someone listened & ACTUALLY made me felt visible. Instead of shutting me down, she acknowledged all of my feelings.

Yes, I fully understand it's not my partner's job to fix me. She's not a certified psychologist, and she is in no way bound to help me.

But because of her, I want to help myself. I don't know how (yet), but I promised myself I'll figure it out soon. I'll book an appointment with the nearest mental clinic soon, and get help. That's everything I know for now.

I understand it won't be this way forever & I'll eventually lose to those thoughts, but I want to keep my hopes up that the next time it comes, I won't burn down in flames.

The good part of Reddit community has been a major part of my journey to survive, and I can never be thankful enough.

I decided to start being better, and I'm hopeful this time it'll last long. To everyone who's been there whether for a simple "hang in there", or a long paragraph giving me advice, you guys are the best people the world has to offer.

:)
text:

The positive side of the Reddit community has played a big role in helping me survive, and I’m deeply grateful.

I’ve decided to work on becoming better, and I hope this time it lasts. To everyone who has supported me—even with a simple “hang in there” or detailed advice—you’re some of the kindest people out there. :)

original_text:
Do you know that feeling when make a decision that you know is wrong? When you try to keep yourself occupied to hide from your thoughts? Like keeping up a wall in your mind?

Long ago I made a decision: My thoughts will never be against me, I will always be safe inside my head. This was necessary because the outside world - mostly my family - was often against me. For a child, this kind of decision is easy to make. 

Nowadays this decision has granted me a super power: Reflection. The luxury to admit my mistakes and faults to myself, and to blame myself for them without dragging myself down. Reflecting and admitting your mistakes is hard. It's a skill you have to practise, but it's a skill worth learning.

Last night I was reflecting, after once again staying up until 5 am, something I promised myself I would stop doing. I figured that my current approach is not working, I reflect but I still put up a wall that blocks the voice in my head that knows what the right thing to do is. Face your thoughts is a phrase i thought of for myself to hopefully start to listen to that voice.

As I write this I realise that my story is very abstract and likely helps nobody. I think reflecting is one of the most important tools for self-development and I hope to put it in better words one day.

text:
I feel like I’ve become too attached to games like Xbox. Recently, I also started my first job, doing a paper round.

Before I go further, I should say that this might sound unstructured, because I don’t really know what I’m aiming for. I don’t fully understand myself. I’m not lost, though—I want to work in pharmaceutical science, advertising, politics, and other fields. It just feels like I don’t really know who I am yet.

There’s a lot of stress too—stress from everything. I can’t play games on my Xbox anymore because they make me anxious, and now I can’t really use my phone either because communication stresses me out. Homework feels impossible because I can’t find motivation, and home life is loud and overwhelming, with constant shouting—either anger or just other people enjoying themselves.

It feels like nothing I do is actually leading anywhere. I want to do more, but I don’t have the motivation.

I even searched: “Why does it feel like I’m doing nothing?”

The answer basically felt like: because I am doing nothing meaningful and just wasting time. I read that anxiety, procrastination, and distraction are all connected, and I think that might be true. But I don’t know how to move past just sitting there doing nothing—wasting time, eating, playing games. It used to feel fun, but now it just feels empty. I want to revise, improve myself, and become better, but I can’t, and that frustrates me.

One reason I can’t revise is the pressure at home. One of my brothers creates a lot of stress around cleaning but doesn’t clean up after himself—leaving pans dirty and knives out. The other is constantly loud, in and out of the house, and ignores what I say completely. I feel like I have no control, and trying to gain it only makes things more stressful because I struggle with communication, especially being the youngest.

I went to my GP with my mum about anxiety and concerns around drug use, which was misunderstood. My brother assumed I was addicted to weed, but I only tried it once and decided not to again for several reasons. I don’t even understand how someone could become addicted to it. After that, I expected some kind of support, but nothing really happened—no therapy, no medication, just being left to manage. I did get school counselling, but even that ended.

So I keep asking myself: why does nothing ever start properly or go well? Why does life feel so pointless? Why does my life feel like it’s failing? If people want to help, why does it still feel like nothing changes? What am I supposed to do to prove I’m worth something? How do I even show that? Maybe people just don’t see anything valuable in me. Maybe I’m the problem. I want to change, but I don’t know how, and everything feels like it gets in the way—anxiety, home life, distractions, my own thoughts.

And I keep coming back to the same question:

Why does it feel like I’m doing nothing, even when I’m trying?
How do I actually do something and still feel okay about it?

Before I fall into an intense state of irritability and depression, I notice that I obsess about the slight irritability I’m experiencing in that moment. Instead of letting it go, I ask myself why do I feel this way? What did I do? This provokes worse thoughts. Maybe I’m bipolar, maybe I’ll never actually be stable. Maybe I’m not made to do anything good, but instead, I’m supposed to be a failure. This all happens within a few day of me noticing the very slight irritability. This compounds my feelings. On top of that—since I’ve been through this before—I become fearful for what’s to come. 

Some other factors that lead up to this moment:
1) increased caffeine consumption 
2) worry and stress due to school

When I become obsessed about my state of being, I can’t think rationally. Instead of simply seeing this as a mental burnout from school covered up by coffee, I start to believe I’m fucked up in the head. This creates fear, fear provokes thoughts, thoughts creates anxiety—I’m sure you guys know the cycle. 

I don’t know how the hell to stop myself from being so damn obsessed with negative thoughts. I ruminate on my unpleasant emotions—that everyone experiences—and I chalk them up to be this evil aspect that dooms me from ever being happy. 

Does anyone else suffer from obsession like this? Or hypersensitivity? What can I do to manage this behavior or even diminish it?

Does anyone else experience this kind of obsession or hypersensitivity? What can be done to manage it or reduce it?

I'm currently in the midst of my second semester of junior year, and I feel like I've entirely failed and messed myself up. It started this time last year as schools announced they would open up in person for the next year, I started feeling extremely anxious and distressed about my future because I was soon going to leave my home and go to uni internationally. I thought I could pick myself up and try to reset from those feelings, but I just keep spiraling everytime I try.

I want to do so many things in life, but I just keep holding myself back. Everytime I push myself to achieve more, I end up messing myself up and disappointing not only myself but my parents as well. This may sound pretentious, but this year I wasn't able to make it into NHS, which I took as a severe hit since my GPA was only 0.07 points short. I legitimately felt like I failed, and when I saw others get in I became extremely upset because I knew I could've done it if I tried. But I couldn't make it since I slacked off and had become lazy due to my anxiety and being overwhelmed by everything.

This "rut" or whatever has seriously been pulling me down by making me irresponsible and negligent and I don't know how to get out of it. I am so upset because I literally feel like all of the opportunities, dreams, and goals I have are just slipping away before I can even reach them. There is so much that I want to change but I don't have enough time left, and my parents have also lost faith in me since they don't feel safe in investing in my future anymore. It's come to the point where I have hidden any of my results from them because of how ashamed I was in myself, and how much I have changed. I get so caught up in what I used to be all the time and I don't know how to go back. I hate myself for who I am now because I can see that I will just be left behind, but I don't know how to catch up.

I've been thinking of seeing someone about this, but unfortunately due to where I live and my families beliefs it isn't possible. Any advice would be awesome.

But for some reason I don't. I've become trapped in my own house, but mainly my room.

I had so many people around me ( which I know now isn't something I need because having a smaller group that really understand me is safer for my mental health ). And now I've learned so much looking back and realizing a lot of what I was doing was trying to fill a void that will never be filled, and that's okay because what is greater than that is finding myself and loving myself. But I just feel like I've quit living and I'm only existing. 

The most I do is work and hit the grocery store, and then I just come home to my room. My home life is totally loving and great by the way, I've just made my room my "Safe haven" but it's no longer working for me. My social group is almost non existent and most of my friends don't live in my state or were formed on the internet ( which isn't a bad thing ), I have no problems being social but I have major issues making a plan and staying committed to it, or cancelling plans continuously to the point where I'm realizing that people don't even bother to ask me anymore ( which I understand, I honestly don't blame them ). But I want to experience the world again. Meet new people. Even find someone that I can spend time and create adventures with. But I'm stuck, and it's gradually gotten worse over the years. It feels like my mental battery has changed OR I've conditioned myself to feel like I can't have fun, something is going to happen, or I don't even deserve to feel this happy...but I do. I deserve to live right? To live a happy life full of experiences.

I’m not really sure where to begin, but I’ll try my best. Finding this group and seeing how openly others talk about their struggles has motivated me to reach out and ask for help and advice, because I’ve never been very good at trusting my own judgment. So here I am.

Over the past few years, I’ve become such a recluse that I can barely imagine leaving my house anymore. A big part of it feels like a need for control—if I stay at home, I can control my environment and what happens around me. But the moment I’m invited to something like dinner or an event, I immediately start looking for reasons not to go. I’ve missed friends’ weddings, baby showers, graduation parties, and many other important events. I know I should feel grateful that people still want me there, but for some reason I don’t. I feel stuck inside my own home, mostly in my room.

I’m 28, but in my teens and early twenties I was constantly out—partying, shopping, going out to eat, always on the move, surrounded by people. I had a large social circle, which I now realize isn’t necessarily what I need; a smaller group that truly understands me is better for my mental health. Looking back, I understand that a lot of what I was doing was trying to fill a void that could never really be filled, and I’m learning that it’s okay. What matters more is finding and accepting myself. But right now it feels like I’ve stopped living and I’m only existing.

Most of my time is spent working, going to the grocery store, and then coming back home to my room. My home life is loving and supportive, and I’ve just turned my room into a “safe space,” but it no longer helps me the way it used to. My social circle is very small now, with most friends living in other states or being people I met online, which isn’t necessarily bad. I don’t have trouble being social, but I struggle a lot with making plans and actually following through. I cancel so often that people don’t really invite me anymore, which I understand and don’t blame them for. Still, I want to experience life again—meet new people, maybe find someone to share experiences with—but I feel stuck, and it’s been getting worse over time.

It feels like my ability to enjoy life has changed, or maybe I’ve trained myself to believe I can’t have fun, that something bad will happen, or that I don’t deserve happiness. But I do believe I deserve to live a happy life full of experiences.

Being so focused on protecting my energy and staying comfortable has slowly pushed me away from life. I can function alone, but the loneliness I feel now tells me something has shifted. I need help figuring out how to re-enter the world—how to commit to plans and believe I deserve good people and good experiences again. Maybe this will resonate with others too. We all deserve that.

I don't know where to start but I'll try my best. Finding this group and seeing others being open and honest about their own struggles has really motivated me to maybe reach out and ask for some help and advice. Because I can never take my own. So, here I go.

I've become such a recluse over the past few years that I'm at a point where I can't even fathom being out of my house. A big part of me thinks it is a control thing. If I'm at home I can control my surroundings and what goes on but the moment I would take someone up on going out to dinner or an event I immediately try to find an excuse to not go. I've missed out on friend's weddings, baby shower's, graduation parties, and all sort of things. And I should feel grateful that people want me to be apart of these things right? But for some reason I don't. I've become trapped in my own house, but mainly my room. 

I'm 28, but when I was in my teen years and early twenties I was out ALL the time. Partying, shopping with friends, going to dinner, and just constantly on the move. I had so many people around me ( which I know now isn't something I need because having a smaller group that really understand me is safer for my mental health ). And now I've learned so much looking back and realizing a lot of what I was doing was trying to fill a void that will never be filled, and that's okay because what is greater than that is finding myself and loving myself. But I just feel like I've quit living and I'm only existing. 

The most I do is work and hit the grocery store, and then I just come home to my room. My home life is totally loving and great by the way, I've just made my room my "Safe haven" but it's no longer working for me. My social group is almost non existent and most of my friends don't live in my state or were formed on the internet ( which isn't a bad thing ), I have no problems being social but I have major issues making a plan and staying committed to it, or cancelling plans continuously to the point where I'm realizing that people don't even bother to ask me anymore ( which I understand, I honestly don't blame them ). But I want to experience the world again. Meet new people. Even find someone that I can spend time and create adventures with. But I'm stuck, and it's gradually gotten worse over the years. It feels like my mental battery has changed OR I've conditioned myself to feel like I can't have fun, something is going to happen, or I don't even deserve to feel this happy...but I do. I deserve to live right? To live a happy life full of experiences. 

Being so high strung on protecting my energy and enjoying my space and time has pushed me away from so much. I can function alone, but when I started to feel lonely is when I knew something in me had started to change. I just need help on how to get back into the living world. How to stay committed to plans and believe that I deserve good people and experiences in my life again. Maybe this will even help others who feel like this too. We ALL deserve it.
text:
I’m not entirely sure how to start, but I’ll do my best. Coming across this group and seeing how openly people share their struggles has encouraged me to reach out for help and advice, since I’ve never been great at trusting my own perspective. So I’m trying.

Over the last few years, I’ve become increasingly reclusive to the point where I can barely imagine leaving my house. A large part of it feels like a control issue—staying at home means I can control my surroundings. But whenever I’m invited out, even to something simple like dinner or an event, I immediately look for a way out. I’ve missed weddings, baby showers, graduations, and many other important moments in people’s lives. I know I should feel grateful to be included, but I don’t always feel that way. I feel like I’ve become trapped inside my house, mostly in my room.

I’m 28, and yet in my teens and early twenties I was constantly out—socializing, partying, shopping, going to restaurants, always active and surrounded by people. I had a large social circle, though I now understand that a smaller, more meaningful group is healthier for me. Looking back, I can see that I was often trying to fill a void that couldn’t really be filled. I’m learning that it’s okay, and that what matters now is understanding and valuing myself. But right now, it feels like I’ve stopped truly living and am just existing.

My daily routine is mostly work, a quick grocery trip, and then staying in my room. My home life is actually loving and stable—I’ve just made my room my comfort zone, but it doesn’t work for me anymore. My social circle is very small now, with most friends living elsewhere or being online connections, which I don’t see as negative. I don’t struggle with being social itself, but I do struggle with making plans and following through. I cancel so often that people have stopped asking me, which I understand. Still, I want to experience life again—meet people, build connections, maybe even share experiences with someone—but I feel stuck, and it seems to be getting worse over time.

It feels like something has changed in my ability to enjoy life, or maybe I’ve conditioned myself into believing I can’t have fun, that something bad will happen, or that I don’t deserve happiness. But I know I do deserve a happy life filled with experiences.

Focusing so much on protecting my energy and comfort has slowly distanced me from life. I can manage on my own, but the loneliness makes it clear that something has shifted. I need help figuring out how to reconnect—how to commit to plans and believe I deserve good people and experiences again. Maybe others feel this too. We all deserve it.

original_text:
It's the classic tale - he was great at the beginning, and then stopped showing affection and effort after a couple of months. Criticized me more than complimented me, yelled at me, etc.

But of course I already loved him. So I communicated the problems - he got mad. We broke up. 

He apologizes, we're on again. Same problem happens, we're off. One time he said he thought he was ready but he was wrong. Two months later, we're on again.

I kept thinking he was going to change - because it really seemed like he wanted to. But I know I'm disrespecting myself by still wanting him.

If I loved myself more, I would not have taken him back after he showed me his true colors. But the issue is I don't. In a way, he made me love myself less. Now I keep wondering what's wrong with me. I dulled myself to be with him. I want to be better, so that the next time he comes around I am capable of saying no and meaning it. 

Because I deserve better - I deserve someone who loves me and does not need to leave to realize it. I deserve someone who loves me even when I struggle to love myself. I deserve someone who respects my boundaries, and does not try to find something to blame me for when I ask for reassurance. 

I also need to stop excusing his actions - I understand he battles with his mental health, but when will I stop letting him mistreat me because of it?

Has anyone else struggled with this? Forgiving someone because you understand them? Or loving someone more than you love yourself?

How did you find the self love to move on? At this point, I'm not sure if it was even there to begin with.
text:
It’s the classic situation—things were great at the start, and then after a couple of months he stopped showing affection and putting in effort. He began criticizing me more than complimenting me, raising his voice at me, and so on.

But I already loved him. So I tried to communicate the issues, and he got angry. We broke up.

Then he apologized, and we got back together. The same problems returned, so we broke up again. At one point he said he thought he was ready for a relationship but realized he wasn’t. A couple of months later, we got back together again.

I kept hoping he would change because it genuinely seemed like he wanted to. But I also know I’m not respecting myself by continuing to want him.

If I loved myself more, I wouldn’t have taken him back after seeing how things really were. The issue is that I don’t. In some ways, being with him made me love myself less. Now I keep questioning what’s wrong with me. I feel like I’ve dimmed myself just to stay with him. I want to grow to the point where I can say no and actually mean it if he comes back again.

Because I do deserve better—I deserve someone who consistently loves me, not someone who needs to leave to realize my worth. I deserve someone who loves me even when I struggle to love myself. I deserve someone who respects my boundaries and doesn’t turn things around on me when I ask for reassurance.

I also need to stop justifying his behavior. I understand he has mental health struggles, but I keep letting that become an excuse for how he treats me.

Has anyone else been through something like this—forgiving someone because you understand them, or loving someone more than yourself?

How did you learn to choose self-love and move on? Right now, I’m not even sure if I had it to begin with.

original_text:
So yea like the title says, I just don't care anymore. And yes, it's because of romantic rejection (I know, extremely juvenile).

The first time I was rejected was when I was 17. That one made a world of difference on me. I worked off half my weight (dropped from 130kg to 67kg), stopped wearing cargo short and metal band tees. got a decent haircut, got decent hobbies. Since then I've been rejected like 5 more times, with the last one being from a close friend, whom I'd known since I was 18. I asked her, she said yes at first and 3 weeks later called me up to say no.

Rejection is part of the game, but I don't know why this one hit so hard. Since then I've just let myself go. I've gained 15kg, I've stopped trying to be the best academically, I wear t-shirts and shorts everywhere. I guess I kinda expected the "no" cos I'm not much...anything, really. I'm not smart, I'm not funny, I'm not interesting. The only thing that keeps me getting out of bed in the morning is the Stoicism I started practicing in 2019. It forces me to keep trucking on at my job since its publicly funded at a gov research facility and therefore a civic duty to produce results.

I want to be better, I want to care about myself and how I look. I want to enjoy my hobbies again, but every time I try, I ask myself what the fucking point is. So yea, I don't know why I'm posting here or what to expect; maybe just to get some general advice, or a nudge in the right direction. I just don't know where to start.
text:

The first time I was rejected I was 17, and it affected me a lot. After that, I made major changes in my life: I lost about half my body weight (from 130kg down to 67kg), stopped wearing cargo shorts and metal band shirts, got a better haircut, and started picking up better hobbies. Since then I’ve been rejected around five more times, the last one being from a close friend I’ve known since I was 18. I asked her out, she initially said yes, but three weeks later she called to say no.

I know rejection is normal, but I don’t understand why this one affected me so strongly. Since then, I’ve kind of let myself go. I’ve gained about 15kg back, stopped putting effort into academics, and now just wear basic t-shirts and shorts everywhere. I guess part of me expected the rejection because I don’t really see myself as much—I'm not particularly smart, funny, or interesting.

The only thing that still keeps me going is Stoicism, which I started practicing in 2019. It pushes me to keep showing up for my job at a government-funded research facility, which I see as a kind of civic responsibility.

I do want to improve. I want to care about myself again, enjoy my hobbies, and take pride in how I look. But whenever I try, I end up wondering what the point is. I’m not really sure why I’m posting this—maybe I just need some advice or a direction, because I don’t know where to start.

text: I struggle to fit in with other men, especially at work. The way they talk and behave often feels hard for me to connect with, even though I’m a man myself. Conversations tend to revolve around the same topics—sports, sex, drinking, joking around, the gym, drugs, video games—and they often use the same slang and tone. I understand that this is how many men bond, but it still feels somewhat forced to me, like people are carefully managing how they come across so nothing seems too intentional or emotional. It seems like they suppress a lot of their natural reactions because spontaneity can be seen as weak or unmanly, so even fear or surprise gets downplayed. I end up feeling disconnected from other men, and I don’t know how to interact with them without pretending to be someone else. I’m not saying this out of bitterness—I think my background, with my father leaving and being raised by a single mother and sisters in a strongly Catholic environment, has influenced why I don’t relate to most men. Still, I feel like something is wrong with me, and I don’t know how to fix it. Sometimes I feel trapped in my own body, limited by social expectations. I don’t feel “manly” enough, though I’m not sure I even want that. I feel like I’m going through an identity crisis, but I also don’t relate to non-binary or trans identities. I don’t want ambiguity either. I wish I could relate more easily to society’s expectations—it would make things simpler and help me feel more accepted.

original_text:
I've struggled with this for years...

I'm sure you too have had the experienced where you promise yourself that by a certain time you will be doing a certain something, then the time comes and the minutes creep over that declared time and before you know it you find yourself negotiating with...yourself to start an hour later

Over the past few months I've experienced some 'little wins' in improving this!

My aim is to practise the discipline of 'dropping that anchor' right on the time I declare and do the thing, just without thinking I try to jolt myself into action before my 'inner negotiator' intervenes

These are small things like washing the dishes at 7pm or when it comes to the weekend and it's a cleaning day, regimenting each task with a time and successively doing it exactly when I plan to do it, on the minute!

I've noticed this skill slowly creeping into other areas of my life like getting out of bed, which is one I've chased for so long!

But anyhow, the lesson I'm trying to convey is the practise of 'dropping that anchor' (by which I mean, doing the thing) on the precise minute you plan to do it - with no slips, delays or abandoning the idea entirely

It's building a habit and a discipline to transfer to other areas, super powerful!

I hope this can be of help to anyone :)
text:
I’ve struggled with this for years.

I’m sure many people can relate to setting a specific time to do something, only for that moment to arrive and slowly pass while you begin negotiating with yourself to delay it “just a little longer.”

Over the last few months, though, I’ve had some small victories improving this habit.

What I’ve been practicing is what I call “dropping the anchor” — meaning that the moment the planned time arrives, I immediately do the task before my mind has a chance to argue against it.

These are simple things, like washing dishes at 7pm or organizing weekend cleaning tasks into scheduled times and completing each one exactly when planned, down to the minute.

I’ve started noticing this discipline carrying over into other parts of my life too, especially getting out of bed, which has been a long-term struggle for me.

The main lesson I’m trying to share is the importance of “dropping the anchor” exactly when you intend to act, without delays, excuses, or abandoning the task altogether.

It’s a habit and form of discipline that can gradually improve many areas of life, and it’s incredibly powerful.

Hopefully this helps someone :)

>If you want to own Rolls Royce, stop taking advice from Toyota owners

&#x200B;

>Smart people learn from everything and everyone, average people from their experiences, stupid people already have all the answers.

Learn from those who have the ability and the experience to teach and you'll see yourself get better.

Thanks for Reading,

Warm Regards,

Arjun Agarwal
text:
This is a story shared by Aman Gupta, co-founder of boAt.

In my childhood, there was a middle-aged man living near our house who had a very unstable career. He frequently lost jobs and would sometimes remain unemployed for months at a time.

Despite this, he was extremely passionate about cricket. Whenever a player failed to score, he would shout at the TV: “What are you doing, man? How did you even make it to the national team? You should’ve played the sweep shot!”

If a player got out, he would lose his temper completely: “Do you have eyes or buttons on your head?”

At the time, I remember thinking, “Uncle, you haven’t had a stable job in eight years, yet you’re advising Sachin Tendulkar, one of the greatest cricketers ever?”

Moral of the story: people are often very good at giving advice without following it themselves. It’s up to us to decide what advice is actually valuable and what should be ignored.

There is also a widely shared quote of uncertain origin:
“If you want to own a Rolls Royce, stop taking advice from Toyota owners.”

However, it’s also not wise to ignore all advice completely. As Socrates is credited with saying:
“Smart people learn from everything and everyone, average people from their experiences, and foolish people already think they know everything.”

The key is to learn from those who truly have experience and knowledge, and you will continue to improve.

Thanks for reading.  
Warm regards,  
Arjun Agarwal

original_text:
I can only change what I am aware of. Sometimes I can’t change things. There’s a interesting balance between these two. Giving up control and taking control. I really have lived in the opposite for so much my life. Trying to control the things that are uncontrollable and not taking control of the things that are completely within my power. 

Realizing the difference is so important but it didn’t come easily. I still struggle with it sometimes but it has gotten much easier with practice. 

What I can control: what I say, what I do, and even what I think. My responses to everything around me. My responses to my own internal environment. I can control those - with practice. 
What I can’t control: other people, other people’s opinions, other people’s words, how people take my responses. 

It’s important to know the difference between what I should focus on and what I shouldn’t. I have gotten stuck in depression, anxiety, and sickness trying to control things that are out of my control. I’ve also gotten stuck in them when I gave up my power in the situations where I had control. 

Let me uncover the things that weigh me down so I can be free from their heaviness. Let me become aware of the things that serve my higher purpose so that I can soar. I choose to let go of expectations, of myself and other people. I choose to mindfully think, speak, and take inspired action. I choose to remain silent when silence is the best option. I choose to respond instead of knee-jerk react when I feel emotional responses. I choose to pause when I need to. 

With these intention, I bright light into places that only darkness used to exist.
text:
I can only change what I’m aware of, and there are also things I simply cannot change. There’s a constant balance between surrendering control and taking responsibility for what is within my power. For much of my life, I’ve lived the opposite way—trying to control things that were never controllable, while neglecting the things I could actually influence.

Understanding the difference between the two hasn’t been easy, but it’s become clearer with time and practice, even though I still struggle with it at times.

What I can control includes my words, my actions, and even my thoughts—my responses to both the external world and my internal experience. With effort, I can shape those responses. What I cannot control includes other people, their opinions, their words, and how they interpret what I do or say.

Knowing where to direct my focus is essential. I’ve fallen into depression, anxiety, and burnout by trying to control what is beyond me, and I’ve also suffered when I failed to take responsibility for what I could control.

I want to uncover what weighs me down so I can release it. I want to recognize what supports my higher purpose so I can move toward it. I choose to let go of expectations—both of myself and others. I choose to think, speak, and act with awareness. I choose silence when it is appropriate. I choose to respond rather than react when emotions arise. I choose to pause when needed.

With these intentions, I bring light into places that once felt only dark.

original_text:
*Just because you have the instructions in front of you doesn't mean you've built the chair.*  


Speaking a lot from my personal experiences recently in case anyone else is in a similar position...  
Over the years I've read article after article, researched different lifestyles, diets, religion(more so the teachings than the beliefs), supplements, medications and of course plenty of 'guru' self-help and most recently, reading. I've learnt a lot and they have improved my life to some extent. I have found as much with therapy in context of improving my life.   


One thing that none of the aforementioned does, however is put in the hours (insert The Rock here). It's one thing to know you have to get up each day early, to start with a good breakfast, to exercise, to get sunlight, to have a balanced diet, take walks, to practise using the tools of Buddhism, psychology, the tools from therapy or ever from redditors that have shared genuinely helpful tips - yet it's another thing entirely to actually do those. It's especially difficult to do them when you're depressed, low on motivation, tired,  fatigued and/or distracted by anything from  intrusive thoughts to the internet. 

TLDR; *This is partly to vent as I am working through this very issue right now (and have been for a few weeks) but also to share a few things that have been helping me the last few days and to ask others here* ***'What has helped you?'***   


***1) Mindfullness;*** Keeping focused on the moment, especially and specifically when we are absorbing information about self-help. I find it immensely helpful to question if I want to be reading self-help at 2am or sleeping (at least trying to/relaxing) and practising it.   


***2) Discomfort;*** Slowly  getting used to discomfort and instead of attempting to eliminate it by having a hot shower instead of a cold, going back to sleep instead of being awake, sitting like a shrimp instead of upright or avoiding exercise because 'uncomfy' - instead of that slowly add something uncomfortable but overall beneficial and mindfully experience that discomfort, remind yourself it's a good thing, even if it's just 1 push up or 1 minute of meditation. Positive reinforcement is great here and the more we get used to discomfort the more we realise that it can be a tool to use in the improvement of our life.  


**3) Forgiveness;** This is one of the hardest but most important (for me, at least). Forgiving ourselves when we take a step back - realising that anyone successful in relationships, careers, skills and life has failed a million times before hand. Life is full of failure, it doesn't make us one. This moment is fresh and new and no matter what happened 5 minutes ago it doesn't define our future, we do. We get to choose to act and be how we want, to create and take the opportunities and if we try our best but fall short of our ideal self, that's ok and it always will be. We'll never be perfect, there's no such thing but we can always be on the path to our ideal self, tripping, falling face first, grazing our knees together in progression.  I have no doubt in my mind that even you reading this has faced challenges and come far - even for being in this sub.   


I really hope this was helpful - even if it's just relatable. There is more I wish to add but it is late for me and I know it is best for me to sleep now so goodnight and again, please feel free to share anything you've found helpful for yourselves!
text:
*Just because you have the instructions doesn’t mean you’ve actually built the chair.*

I’m speaking a bit from personal experience here, in case anyone else is going through something similar.

Over the years, I’ve read countless articles, researched different lifestyles, diets, religious teachings (more the philosophy than the belief systems), supplements, medications, and plenty of self-help “guru” content. More recently, I’ve also been reading more books. I’ve learned a lot, and many of these things have improved my life to some extent, including therapy.

However, one thing none of this provides is the actual hours of doing. It’s one thing to know you should wake up early, eat a good breakfast, exercise, get sunlight, maintain a balanced diet, go for walks, and apply tools from Buddhism, psychology, therapy, or even helpful advice from Reddit—but it’s an entirely different thing to actually do them. This becomes even harder when you’re depressed, unmotivated, tired, fatigued, or distracted by intrusive thoughts and the internet.

**TLDR:** I’m partly venting because I’m actively dealing with this issue right now (and have been for weeks), but also sharing a few things that have helped recently—and asking others: *what has helped you?*

**1) Mindfulness:**  
Staying present, especially when consuming self-help content. I find it useful to ask myself whether I really want to be reading self-help at 2am or if I should be sleeping, resting, or relaxing instead—and then actually practicing that awareness.

**2) Discomfort:**  
Gradually getting used to discomfort instead of avoiding it—choosing a cold shower over a hot one, staying awake instead of going back to sleep, sitting properly instead of slouching, or doing exercise even when it feels uncomfortable. Even starting with something small like one push-up or one minute of meditation helps. The goal is to experience discomfort mindfully, remind yourself it’s beneficial, and build tolerance. Over time, discomfort becomes something you can use to improve your life.

**3) Forgiveness:**  
This is one of the hardest but most important parts for me. Forgiving ourselves when we slip up, and remembering that anyone successful has failed countless times before. Failure is part of life, not our identity. The present moment is always new, and what happened minutes ago doesn’t define what comes next—we do. We can choose to act differently and move toward the person we want to become. Even if we fall short, that’s okay. Perfection doesn’t exist, but progress does—even if it’s messy and unsteady. I believe everyone reading this has already overcome more than they realize, simply by being here.

I hope this was helpful, or at least relatable. It’s late for me, so I’m going to sleep now—but I’d genuinely love to hear what has helped others.

text:
*Having instructions in front of you doesn’t mean the chair is built.*

I’m drawing from personal experience here in case someone relates.

Over the years I’ve consumed a lot of self-improvement content—articles, lifestyle research, diet changes, religious teachings (more philosophical than doctrinal), supplements, medication discussions, self-help gurus, and recently more books. I’ve learned a great deal, and some of it has improved my life, including therapy.

But none of this actually does the work for you. Knowing you should wake up early, eat well, exercise, get sunlight, walk, meditate, or apply psychological tools is very different from actually doing them. And doing them becomes especially difficult when you’re low on energy, depressed, distracted, or stuck in intrusive thoughts.

**TLDR:** I’m partly venting because I’m going through this right now, but also sharing what’s been helping me recently—and asking: *what has helped you?*

**1) Mindfulness:**  
Staying aware in the moment, especially when consuming self-help content. Asking whether I should be scrolling self-improvement at 2am or sleeping instead has been very grounding.

**2) Discomfort:**  
Instead of avoiding discomfort, I try to gently include it—cold showers instead of warm, staying awake instead of drifting back to sleep, better posture instead of slouching, or doing small amounts of exercise even when I don’t want to. Even something minimal like one push-up or a minute of meditation helps. The idea is to experience discomfort consciously and reframe it as something useful.

**3) Forgiveness:**  
This is the most difficult one for me. Learning to forgive myself after setbacks and remembering that success always includes failure. Mistakes don’t define who we are. Every moment is a reset, and the past doesn’t determine the future unless we let it. We can choose our direction again and again. Even imperfect effort still counts. No one becomes their ideal self without repeatedly failing along the way.

I hope this resonates with someone. I’d love to hear what others have found helpful as well.

original_text:
*Just because you have the instructions in front of you doesn't mean you've built the chair.*  


Speaking a lot from my personal experiences recently in case anyone else is in a similar position...  
Over the years I've read article after article, researched different lifestyles, diets, religion(more so the teachings than the beliefs), supplements, medications and of course plenty of 'guru' self-help and most recently, reading. I've learnt a lot and they have improved my life to some extent. I have found as much with therapy in context of improving my life.   


One thing that none of the aforementioned does, however is put in the hours (insert The Rock here). It's one thing to know you have to get up each day early, to start with a good breakfast, to exercise, to get sunlight, to have a balanced diet, take walks, to practise using the tools of Buddhism, psychology, the tools from therapy or ever from redditors that have shared genuinely helpful tips - yet it's another thing entirely to actually do those. It's especially difficult to do them when you're depressed, low on motivation, tired,  fatigued and/or distracted by anything from  intrusive thoughts to the internet. 

TLDR; *This is partly to vent as I am working through this very issue right now (and have been for a few weeks) but also to share a few things that have been helping me the last few days and to ask others here* ***'What has helped you?'***   


***1) Mindfullness;*** Keeping focused on the moment, especially and specifically when we are absorbing information about self-help. I find it immensely helpful to question if I want to be reading self-help at 2am or sleeping (at least trying to/relaxing) and practising it.   


***2) Discomfort;*** Slowly  getting used to discomfort and instead of attempting to eliminate it by having a hot shower instead of a cold, going back to sleep instead of being awake, sitting like a shrimp instead of upright or avoiding exercise because 'uncomfy' - instead of that slowly add something uncomfortable but overall beneficial and mindfully experience that discomfort, remind yourself it's a good thing, even if it's just 1 push up or 1 minute of meditation. Positive reinforcement is great here and the more we get used to discomfort the more we realise that it can be a tool to use in the improvement of our life.  


**3) Forgiveness;** This is one of the hardest but most important (for me, at least). Forgiving ourselves when we take a step back - realising that anyone successful in relationships, careers, skills and life has failed a million times before hand. Life is full of failure, it doesn't make us one. This moment is fresh and new and no matter what happened 5 minutes ago it doesn't define our future, we do. We get to choose to act and be how we want, to create and take the opportunities and if we try our best but fall short of our ideal self, that's ok and it always will be. We'll never be perfect, there's no such thing but we can always be on the path to our ideal self, tripping, falling face first, grazing our knees together in progression.  I have no doubt in my mind that even you reading this has faced challenges and come far - even for being in this sub.   


I really hope this was helpful - even if it's just relatable. There is more I wish to add but it is late for me and I know it is best for me to sleep now so goodnight and again, please feel free to share anything you've found helpful for yourselves!

*Just because you’ve read the instructions doesn’t mean the chair is actually built.*

This is partly from my own experience in case it helps anyone else going through something similar.

Over the years I’ve read a huge amount about self-improvement—articles, lifestyle research, diets, religion (mainly teachings and philosophy), supplements, medications, self-help content, and more recently books. I’ve learned a lot from all of it, and it has improved my life somewhat, including through therapy.

But none of it replaces actual consistent action. Knowing what you “should” do—wake up early, eat properly, exercise, get sunlight, walk daily, use psychological tools, apply advice from therapy or even online communities—is very different from actually doing it. And doing it becomes especially hard when motivation is low, when you’re tired, depressed, or mentally distracted.

**TLDR:** I’m partly venting because I’m dealing with this issue right now, but also sharing a few things that have helped me recently—and asking others: *what has worked for you?*

**1) Mindfulness:**  
Being aware in the present moment, especially when consuming self-help information. Checking whether I really want to be reading this stuff late at night or whether I should be sleeping has been helpful.

**2) Discomfort:**  
Training myself to tolerate discomfort instead of avoiding it—choosing cold showers over warm ones, staying up instead of going back to sleep, correcting posture instead of slouching, or doing small exercises even when it feels uncomfortable. Even tiny actions like one push-up or one minute of meditation help build the habit. Over time, discomfort becomes something you can work with rather than avoid.

**3) Forgiveness:**  
Probably the hardest one for me. Learning to forgive myself when I slip and remembering that everyone successful has failed repeatedly. Failure is universal and doesn’t define identity. Each moment is a new opportunity to act differently, regardless of what happened before. We can always choose again. Even imperfect progress still matters, because perfection doesn’t exist—only direction does.

I hope this was useful or at least relatable. I’m heading to sleep now, but I’d genuinely be interested to hear what others have found helpful.

original_text:
I don’t have it figured out .... at all. 

AND it’s okay. It’s actually a wonderful thing even when it feels really bad. We’re not supposed to have it all figured out, and if someone says they do, I think it’s safe to say they’re lying. 

I was a mess today. I have a day off from work as I just got back from a trip yesterday. My trip was for my best friends bachelorette and it was amazing. I had my moments where I was sad because the trip came up 5 days after I returned home from Florida after my dad passed away. It was a nice distraction and I was more than thrilled to celebrate my best friend and her upcoming wedding. But this morning I had a set back. I hadn’t planned out something and I took a trip to Long Island for no reason and had to then right back around. I was beating myself up because it was avoidable but the truth of the matter is I’m still not grounded. I’m grieving and it hurts. I’ve been running around since he passed away and today the universe told me it’s time to stop. It’s time to sit and do nothing. It’s time to cry and feel and hurt and be loving to myself. 

Each time I have a big AHA moment, I kind of forget about it. I’ve focused on self care so many occasions but I haven’t integrated it into my life and now is the time. I’m learning self-care is a lifestyle, not a few actions daily. I beat myself up A LOT. I expect perfection from myself even though I constantly write that no ones perfect. I know and understand so many things but I have a hard time BEING them. There’s a huge difference between them. 

My dads passing was really traumatic. I watched him take his last breath after 24 hours of him suffering through his breathing. I watched him go through an illness for years and even though it was expected, his death didn’t seem peaceful like my mom and I desperately wanted. I begged God and all my guardians and all of his family members who passed to help him let go of his body. I cried and pleaded so hard in those last 24 hours. 

I’ve decided it’s time to ask for help. I’m so used to giving advice and helping people I forgot that I need help sometimes too. I don’t have it all figured out. I’m not perfect. I’m allowed to break. I’m allowed to cry. I’m allowed to be a mess. And I’m surrendering. I finally understand what that means. 

This is a message to all those who think you need to have it figured out, you don’t. There’s people who seem like life is all positive and even though they may be positive (like me) there’s so many things underneath the surface. I’m still dealing with repressed grief from the passing of my first dad when i was 5. I have no memories from most of my childhood because of it and my Dads passing is bringing so much of it to the surface. I need help and it’s okay. Do what you have to do for you. I’m going to research therapists in the area, one that fits my needs (my mom and her therapist meditate together which I think is awesome lol). It’s time to take care of you.
text:
I don’t have things figured out… not even close.

And that’s okay. In fact, it can be something beautiful even when it feels painful. We’re not meant to have everything figured out, and if someone claims they do, it’s probably not true.

Today I was a mess. I had a day off after just returning from a trip yesterday. That trip was for my best friend’s bachelorette party, and it was incredible. At times I felt sadness because it happened only five days after I came back from Florida, where my dad had passed away. It was a welcome distraction, and I was genuinely happy to celebrate my friend’s upcoming wedding. But this morning I had a setback. I didn’t plan my day properly, ended up driving to Long Island for no reason, and had to turn back. I was hard on myself because it felt avoidable, but the truth is I’m still not grounded. I’m grieving, and it hurts. I’ve been constantly moving since he passed, and today felt like the universe telling me to stop. To sit still. To do nothing. To cry. To feel. To hurt. And to be kind to myself.

Every time I have a big realization, I seem to forget it again. I’ve tried focusing on self-care many times, but I haven’t fully integrated it into my life yet. Now I understand it needs to be a lifestyle, not just occasional actions. I judge myself harshly. I expect perfection even though I know no one is perfect. I understand many things intellectually, but living them is something else entirely.

My dad’s passing was deeply traumatic. I watched him take his last breath after 24 hours of struggling to breathe. I had already seen him battle illness for years, and even though we knew it was coming, his death wasn’t peaceful the way my mom and I hoped. I begged God and any spiritual presence I could think of to help him let go. I cried and pleaded through those final hours.

I’ve decided it’s time to ask for help. I’m used to helping others, and I forgot that I need support too. I don’t have everything figured out. I’m not perfect. I’m allowed to fall apart. I’m allowed to cry. I’m allowed to struggle. And I’m learning what surrender really means.

This is a reminder: you don’t need to have it all figured out. Even people who seem positive or put together have things going on beneath the surface. I still carry unresolved grief from losing my first father when I was five, which erased much of my early childhood memory. My recent loss has brought a lot of that back up. I need help, and that’s okay. Take care of yourself. I’m going to look for a therapist that fits my needs and actually support myself properly now.

text:
I don’t have my life figured out at all.

And that’s okay—actually, there’s something kind of meaningful about that, even when it feels awful. We aren’t supposed to have everything under control, and anyone who claims they do probably doesn’t.

Today was rough for me. I had a day off after returning yesterday from a trip to my best friend’s bachelorette, which was amazing. There were moments of sadness too, since it came just five days after I got back from Florida where my dad passed away. The trip helped distract me, and I was truly happy to celebrate her. But this morning I slipped up—I didn’t plan my day, ended up driving to Long Island for no reason, and had to turn back. I got frustrated with myself because it was avoidable, but I realize I’m still not grounded. I’m grieving, and it hurts. I’ve been constantly on the move since he died, and today felt like a forced pause. A reminder to stop, rest, cry, feel everything, and be gentle with myself.

I keep having moments of realization and then losing them again. I’ve tried practicing self-care before, but I haven’t fully made it part of my life. Now I see it has to be a way of living, not just actions I do sometimes. I tend to be very hard on myself and expect perfection, even though I know logically no one is perfect. I understand many things, but actually embodying them is much harder.

My dad’s death was traumatic. I watched him take his final breath after a long struggle with breathing over 24 hours. He had been ill for years, and even though we knew it was coming, it still wasn’t peaceful like we hoped. I begged and prayed for him to be released from his suffering.

I’ve decided I need help. I’m so used to supporting others that I forget I need support too. I don’t have everything figured out. I’m allowed to break down. I’m allowed to cry. I’m allowed to struggle. I’m learning how to surrender.

This is also a reminder to anyone reading: you don’t need everything figured out. Even people who seem positive have internal struggles. I still carry grief from losing my first father when I was five, which erased much of my childhood memory. My recent loss has brought a lot of that pain back up. I need support, and that’s okay. I’m going to look for a therapist and start taking care of myself in a real way.

original_text:
"*There is nothing either good or bad, but thinking makes it so*." -**Shakespeare in** ***Hamlet***

Do not become your emotions. This sounds like some sort of Jedi saying, so what exactly do I mean by it?  I mean do not let your emotions be so strong that they control you and your actions. When you are filled with rage, feel threatened, or depressed, your actions will be different than if you are in a neutral mental state. You are intoxicated. Do not make major decisions in these moments, do not stray from your normal path, and hold your tongue if you are about to say anything that hurts others.

Let's take a detour to a story about me: When I was 5, I got angry all the time. Since I got angry all the time, I was the best person to pick on in school. I was nicknamed the Hulk. This nickname hurt and I decided to stop being angry. Every time something should have bothered me, I just let it go and let people know that it did not bother me. If I actually was angry, I would not let it affect my actions. When I was 19 I worked in a casual fast food chain with my brother. My boss asked if I ever got upset and bothered by anything since she never saw me upset or bothered. He said, "Not really." I won't forget this moment. Do I get irritated and frustrated? Of course. Do I act on these feelings? Almost never.

&#x200B;

So how can you do this yourself? Your goal is not to stop anger but to reduce it, so that your behavior is within your control.

1. Decide that is not who you are any more. If you believe you cannot change, you have decided your own fate.
2. When you feel the emotion such as anger, notice it. Focus on it. Say "This is anger." It is much easier to control your emotions when you can identify them first. It gives you time to detach and not act reactively.
3. There is always a thought or interpretation that caused you to be angry. A slight by someone. Injustice. An attack to your identity. Or a truth that you wish were not true. **Do not replay this story in your head over and over.** It is the interpretation of the story that is making you angry. It is really hard to stay angry when you are focusing entirely on your emotion. Try to stay angry for 5 minutes without repeatedly thinking about the narrative you tell yourself.  
i.e. Someone rejects you. You feel inadequate. The idea that you may be inadequate makes you angry. The leap to feeling inadequate is just an insecurity you have, which may be based in truth or not. If it is based in truth, that's great! You can control it and make yourself better. That's why we are all here. If it's not, you can think about it and realize that it's just an insecurity that is not based in truth.  Remember, all that happened was someone said, "no."
4. Do not act on your anger, sadness, or depression. If you think there is a .001% chance you will regret an action while you are angry, you will certainly regret it. If you are around the people you love and do not want to hurt them, separate yourself physically or put away your phone so you cannot make a bad decision. Do the things that you would advise your friends to do or do what you would do if you knew you were being recorded.

I know many people will say that anger is appropriate in times of injustice, but that is not the main point of my message. My main goal is to give you the tools to control your emotions rather than them controlling you. If mistreatment of people causes you anger and that anger gives you the impetus to act, that's great.

I got in trouble with the mods for linking to my site previously. If you'd like a pdf version of this article or to see similar posts to this, send me a message.

&#x200B;
text:
*“There is nothing either good or bad, but thinking makes it so.”* — Shakespeare, *Hamlet*

Don’t become your emotions. This may sound like a Jedi-style idea, but what I mean is simple: don’t let your emotions grow so strong that they take control of your actions. When you feel rage, fear, or depression, your behavior naturally shifts compared to when you’re in a calm, neutral state. In those moments, you’re essentially “intoxicated” emotionally. Avoid making major decisions, don’t abandon your usual path, and hold back words that could hurt others.

Let me share a personal example. When I was five, I used to get angry constantly. Because of that, I became an easy target at school and was nicknamed “the Hulk.” That hurt me, and I decided I didn’t want to be an angry person anymore. So whenever something upset me, I chose to let it go and act like it didn’t affect me. Even when I did feel anger, I made sure it didn’t control my actions.

Later, when I was 19, I worked at a casual fast food place with my brother. My boss once asked if I ever got upset or bothered by anything, because she had never seen me react emotionally. I simply said, “Not really.” That moment stayed with me. I do feel irritation and frustration, of course—but I almost never act on those feelings.

So how do you do this?

The goal is not to eliminate anger, but to reduce its influence so your behavior stays under your control:

1. Decide this is not who you are anymore. If you believe change isn’t possible, you’ve already limited yourself.
2. When an emotion arises, notice it and name it: “This is anger.” Awareness creates distance between you and the reaction, making control easier.
3. Every strong emotion has a story behind it—an insult, injustice, threat to identity, or uncomfortable truth. Don’t replay that story repeatedly in your mind. The interpretation is what fuels the emotion. Try focusing only on the feeling itself; it becomes harder to sustain anger that way.
   Example: If someone rejects you, you may feel inadequate. That thought may trigger anger. But the rejection is just “no.” The meaning you attach to it is what creates suffering. If the belief is accurate, you can improve yourself. If it’s not, it’s just insecurity.
4. Do not act while emotionally overwhelmed. If there’s even a tiny chance you’ll regret it later, you probably will. Step away physically or remove your ability to act impulsively—put down your phone, leave the space, and behave as you would if you were being observed or advising a friend.

Anger can sometimes motivate action in response to injustice, and that’s not the point I’m arguing against. The goal is simply to ensure emotions don’t control you—you control them.
