text:
*“There is nothing either good or bad, but thinking makes it so.”* — Shakespeare, *Hamlet*

Don’t let yourself become your emotions. It might sound like a philosophical or “Jedi-like” idea, but it simply means this: don’t allow emotions to become so intense that they dictate your behavior. When you’re angry, scared, or depressed, your decisions and reactions change drastically compared to when you’re calm. In those states, you’re not fully in control. You’re emotionally “intoxicated.” That’s why you shouldn’t make big decisions, deviate from your normal direction, or speak in ways you might regret.

A personal story might explain this better. As a child, I was very angry—constantly. Because of that, I became an easy target at school and was nicknamed “the Hulk.” That label stuck with me and eventually pushed me to change. I decided I didn’t want anger controlling me anymore. So whenever something triggered me, I let it pass and acted like it didn’t affect me. Even when anger was present, I didn’t let it drive my behavior.

Years later, when I was 19, I worked in a fast food restaurant with my brother. My manager once asked if anything ever bothered me, because she never saw me upset. I answered, “Not really.” I still remember that moment clearly. I do experience irritation and frustration—but I rarely act on them.

How can you apply this?

The aim isn’t to erase anger but to reduce its control over you:

1. Decide you are no longer the person ruled by these emotions. Believing you can’t change locks you in place.
2. When you feel an emotion, recognize it clearly: “This is anger.” Naming it creates space between feeling and reaction.
3. Every emotional reaction comes from a story you tell yourself—about disrespect, injustice, or insecurity. Don’t replay that story over and over. The narrative fuels the emotion. If you stay focused on the raw feeling, it becomes harder for anger to persist.
   For example, rejection might lead you to feel unworthy. That feeling becomes anger. But the actual event is simply someone saying “no.” The meaning you attach is the real trigger.
4. Never act in a heightened emotional state if there’s even a small chance you’ll regret it. Step away, pause, or remove yourself from the situation. Do what you would do if someone else were watching your actions.

Anger can sometimes serve a purpose, especially when responding to injustice. The goal here isn’t suppression—it’s control.

I (23f) have only been in two relationships because they make me uncomfortable. It's hard for me to trust people and I now understand it's because of my toxic upbringing. My mom is a narcissist and I learned to never confide in my her or tell her anything because she'd either use it against me later to belittle me, tell me I was being too sensitive and I just wanted attention or that I didn't actually feel that way. Now this defense mechanism is my way of life and it's screwing up my relationships. I don't like confrontation so I'm always agreeable. I barely have any boundaries set in place so I'm always saying yes. On the occasions I do say no I feel guilty and think about it for the remainder of the day thinking that person doesn't like me now because of it. Im also very uncomfortable with physical affection. I want nothing more than to have a partner but I'm just so afraid of being judged that I withdraw and clam up which comes off as me not being interested. This has driven every guy that initially was really interested in me away. I don't know how to be open and let my guard down without being so afraid of how they perceive me. This is only in romantic relationships too. What's sad is that once I start trusting my partner and getting ready to open up they are already tired of me and end up leaving.

Tldr: I have defense mechanisms that developed during toxic upbringing which is now negatively effecting my romantic relationships. I don't know how to be more open with my partner without feeling like I'm being judged.
text:
I’m a 23-year-old woman and I’ve only been in two relationships because I tend to feel uncomfortable in them. I struggle with trust, and I’ve come to understand that this comes from my upbringing. My mom is a narcissist, and growing up I learned not to confide in her because anything I shared would later be used against me—either to belittle me, tell me I was too sensitive, accuse me of seeking attention, or deny my feelings altogether.

Because of that, I developed defense mechanisms that I still use today, and they’re now affecting my romantic relationships. I avoid confrontation, so I tend to be overly agreeable. I don’t set many boundaries and often say yes to things even when I don’t want to. When I do say no, I feel guilty and spend the rest of the day worrying that the other person now dislikes me.

I’m also very uncomfortable with physical affection. More than anything, I want a relationship, but I’m so afraid of being judged that I shut down and withdraw, which makes me seem uninterested. This has caused people who were initially interested in me to lose interest and leave. I don’t know how to open up or let my guard down without worrying about how I’m being perceived. This pattern only happens in romantic relationships.

What hurts most is that by the time I start trusting someone and feel ready to open up, they’ve often already pulled away and left.

TL;DR: My childhood created defense mechanisms that now negatively affect my romantic relationships, and I struggle to open up without fearing judgment.

I have started to bring about a few changes in my life a few months ago. More details here - <URL> if someone would like to get some background.

And at times I just feel very overwhelmed and scared.

Scared that all my effort, which I never put in my life before, can still go to waste.

Scared that deciding to put in this much effort might be a huge mistake because what I am trying to achieve might turn out to be something I am either not good at or something I will fail at.

Scared and overwhelmed that I might go through all of the above (in the linked post) again in my life and still fail and again have to start my career and life from scratch (if I have the will to do that after this at all). I have a small window to aim at the things I wish to do, and it's getting to me because I don't know what I would do if I don't manage to complete it within this window. And I don't feel I have any alternatives anymore.

Does anyone else feel this at times? How do you deal with it? 

The above feelings have been recurring but today they felt a lot stronger so hoped to share here and gain some insights on handling it better. 

My therapy a few months ago was more on the CBT side of things, but I stopped going after a while. But the therapist talked about how my brain would actively push forward negative thoughts more when I was making or trying to make progress for the better. But I just don't know how to handle it better while I keep pushing those negative thoughts away.

Afraid that all the effort I’m putting in—something I’ve never really done before in my life—might still end up being wasted.

A few months ago I went through CBT-focused therapy, but I eventually stopped. My therapist mentioned that my mind tends to generate more negative thoughts when I’m trying to improve my life. I just don’t know how to manage those thoughts effectively while still trying to move forward.

Always been a very non-confrontational person, “go with the flow”, but kinda push-overy. 

When I got my first place entirely on my own out of college, they had me pay the higher deposit because I lacked rental history. Through college I had rented, but it was hard to get the paper work together because of various land lords and I was on the lease in some but not the others and just all over the place, so I kinda got it. 

But now, two years later, I’ve been in a steady great job, and started a business. I wanted to move up in apartment a bit, and was waiting on the background check. Their website clearly stated to qualify for a $500 deposit vs $1500, you had to show 1 year rent and meet their income requirements. 

At first they came back and said I needed to pay the higher one, because they couldn’t verify rent history. I had had it, I was tired of being treated like a college student, I came this far so I wouldn’t have to do stuff like this. I immediately sent a pointed email to the rental agency, and copied my landlord. I also emailed the landlord, and copied the company. They both blamed the other party, but within a week they offered me a new lease with the $500 deposit.

Finally stood my ground a bit, held people to higher standards, and saved myself some money! I know it’s just a deposit, but who knows how much of it I’ll get back, and it makes it so much easier to move with less money tied up in that. 

“Don’t you try and go through life worried whether people like you or not, you best be sure they’re doing right by you”
text:
I’ve always been a very non-confrontational person—more of a “go with the flow” type, sometimes to the point of being a bit of a pushover.

When I got my first apartment on my own after college, I was required to pay a higher deposit because I didn’t have strong rental history. During college I had rented before, but my paperwork was messy since I had different landlords, wasn’t consistently on leases, and overall my record was all over the place, so I understood why they did it.

Fast forward two years, I now have a stable well-paying job and have even started a business. I wanted to move into a better apartment and was waiting on the background check. Their website clearly said that to qualify for a $500 deposit instead of $1500, you needed at least one year of rental history and to meet income requirements.

At first, they told me I still had to pay the higher deposit because they couldn’t verify my rental history. I’d had enough—I was tired of being treated like I was still a student. I came this far so I wouldn’t have to deal with situations like this anymore. I immediately sent a firm email to the rental company and CC’d my landlord, and also emailed the landlord while CC’ing the company. They each blamed the other, but within a week I was offered a new lease with the $500 deposit.

It felt good to finally stand my ground, hold people accountable, and save some money in the process. Even though it’s “just a deposit,” it matters—it could be a lot of money returned later, and it makes moving much easier when less cash is tied up.

“Don’t go through life worrying whether people like you—make sure they’re treating you right.”

text:
Long story short, my anxiety levels are currently higher than usual (normally it doesn’t interfere with my daily life). I get triggered more easily, and sometimes once I’m in an anxiety spike, it feels like I stay anxious just for the sake of it, making it hard to calm down.

My biggest trigger right now is a reluctance—and almost fear—of getting close to people. So even though I enjoy spending time with my usual group of friends, I often feel anxious afterward. This is especially true with one specific person whose personality I like, but I have no intention of pursuing a relationship with him. First, I don’t feel anything romantic for him (though I worry I might develop feelings), and second, I’m not interested in dating anyone right now—I just want to focus on myself. At the same time, I don’t want to cut him off because he’s a genuinely good person.

My dilemma is that most of my anxiety comes from interacting with him, and I also find myself withdrawing from my friends in general because of the anxiety. So I wonder—if I take time away from them, am I just avoiding the issue and making my anxiety worse?

I’ve started meditating daily for about a week, going on walks, and trying to think more positively. These help with smaller spikes of anxiety. But last night I had a very intense, uncontrollable episode that made me feel like I needed a few days alone to reset. Earlier this month, when I had a similar situation, taking a break from my online friends seemed to help. However, I’m now worried that because my main triggers involve deeper emotional closeness, avoiding them might actually reinforce the anxiety instead of helping it.

What do you think is the best way to approach healing in this situation?

(I should also mention I have other stressors right now, like job searching, home renovations, and being on my period, so I know these are likely intensifying my anxiety as well.)

original_text:
I feel like I’ve just been coasting for the better part of my life. Just doing the bare minimum if not less. I feel horrible and I have low self esteem. I’m sick of it.

I laze around a lot of the day. Haven’t gotten a full time job (I do some freelance graphic design and work part time at a coffee shop to pay the bills). I binge eat, smoke weed, bullshit around watching useless youtube videos and tv shows and go to bed at like 4am only to wake up late and feel disgusted with myself. I’m fed up with this unproductive and sad lifestyle.
	
The other day I watched Dead Poets Society again, and it just made me so stoked on life. It really made me want to seize the day and do something profound. I felt so incredibly motivated the day after, but that inspiration dwindled a few days later. How can I reinvigorate that state of mind, and continue to stoke that fire? I want to live fully, I want to experience all I am meant to experience and then some. I’m afraid of regret and afraid of not living to my potential. 

I want to make something of myself. I want to continue making artwork, push forward my career, develop a more stable financial situation, stick to a consistent exercise schedule, and just generally feel good about myself. I just want to live better and with intention. Where do I start? How can I get my act together?



*“I went to the woods because I wished to live deliberately, to front only the essential facts of life, and see if I could not learn what it had to teach, and not, when I came to die, discover that I had not lived. I did not wish to live what was not life, living is so dear; nor did I wish to practice resignation, unless it was quite necessary. **I wanted to live deep and suck out all the marrow of life**, to live so sturdily and Spartan-like as to put to rout all that was not life, to cut a broad swath and shave close, to drive life into a corner, and reduce it to its lowest terms...”*
text:
I feel like I’ve spent most of my life just coasting—doing the bare minimum or even less. My self-esteem is really low, and I feel awful about it. I’m tired of living this way.

Most of my days are wasted. I haven’t gotten a full-time job yet (I do some freelance graphic design and work part-time at a coffee shop to pay the bills), but outside of that I mostly just drift. I binge eat, smoke weed, waste time on meaningless YouTube videos and TV shows, and stay up until around 4am, only to wake up late feeling disgusted with myself. I’m really fed up with this unproductive, unhappy routine.

Recently I rewatched *Dead Poets Society*, and it completely reignited something in me. It made me want to “seize the day” and do something meaningful with my life. I felt incredibly motivated the next day, but that feeling faded after a few days. I want to know how to keep that mindset alive—how to maintain that inner fire. I want to live fully, experience everything I’m meant to experience and more. I’m scared of regret and of never reaching my potential.

I want to become someone I’m proud of. I want to keep creating art, build my career, stabilize my finances, exercise consistently, and generally feel better about myself. I want to live with intention. But I don’t know where to start or how to get my life together.

*“I went to the woods because I wished to live deliberately…”* (excerpt from *Walden*) reflects exactly how I want to live—to truly experience life deeply, not just drift through it.

text:
Hi everyone, I don’t usually post about myself, but this thought came up and I figured it might resonate with someone.

A few years ago, I realized how often I would think of doing something kind or helpful, but then overthink it and end up not doing it. By the time I decided, the moment had already passed. It was usually small things—holding a door, offering a quick hand, simple gestures. Living in a city, these situations come up often (though I’ve also realized acting impulsively can sometimes backfire).

I made a personal rule: if I think of something good to do and notice I’m overthinking it, I force myself to just do it. I’ve been improving. The first time I really noticed it was at work during a social setting. We were sitting in a group, and there was a girl alone, so I asked her to join us. The old version of me would have thought, “If she wanted to join, she would,” but I realized that doesn’t mean I shouldn’t invite her.

Since then, I’ve kept practicing this and I feel a bit more genuine and free. I was already fairly comfortable with myself, but now I feel even more liberated. One moment that stood out was when I witnessed a hit and run. I alerted a nearby parking officer, took photos, went to the restaurant I was headed to, asked for a pen and paper, and left a note at the scene offering my contact information in case it helped. I hope it did.

What really inspired me was a moment with a coworker (older than me). While driving, he suddenly said, “That guy just hit that lady!” and immediately got out of the car and ran down the block to intervene (it turned out to be two men). In that moment, I remember thinking I want to be like that—someone who just does the right thing without hesitation. Nothing else about him mattered except that instinct.

I don’t really have a perfect ending, but I hope someone finds this meaningful. I want to be more like the good people I’ve met—and become a better version of myself in the process.

text:
For about the past year, I’ve felt like I’ve been stuck in a slump. I’ve been unhappy with how much I procrastinate and also unhappy with my weight. I kept hoping things would just improve on their own, but of course that didn’t happen.

Over the summer, I tried loosely tracking my food and doing some exercise, but I didn’t stick with it for long and I ended up being really hard on myself. Now that I’m in college, I thought I wouldn’t have time anyway—there was always something to do and I was constantly tired.

This past week, I realized I needed to make real changes. I started by trying to structure my entire day very strictly, even scheduling meals, but that didn’t work for me. Even with buffer time, I would always run over.

So I joined a few productivity communities on Reddit and Discord and started posting daily task lists. Even in just a few days, it’s helped a lot. I don’t always complete everything, but having it visible makes me feel accountable, like I want to finish tasks so I can report that I did them. It feels like a slow but positive shift.

I also wanted to fix my sleep schedule. Some nights I wouldn’t sleep until 2am and then wake up at 6:30am. After reading some articles, I realized that even if better sleep cuts into study time, I’d likely be more productive overall. So I set a goal to be asleep between 11pm and 12am, or within about 30 minutes of that range, and to wake up between 6 and 7am. It’s not a full 8 hours, but it’s more consistent.

This has been challenging—I’ve only managed it about 3 out of 5 days—but I’m planning to keep working on it.

The part I’m happiest about is improving my health habits. I’m a picky eater, mostly because of texture or taste, and while I try to be open, I struggle. I read a post suggesting that it’s more important to meet calorie needs even if food variety is limited. Still, I wanted to improve my diet, so I set a goal to include at least one fruit or vegetable with a meal each week, gradually increasing it.

Yesterday, I even managed to eat half a salad on my own. I also got up this morning and did some simple bodyweight exercises like wall pushups, planks, lunges, and squats. These are small things, but accomplishing them felt really good and motivating. I also have more chances to walk throughout the day.

Anyway, I know this was long, but I just wanted to share. I still have a lot to improve (especially studying habits), but these small changes already make me feel better and more motivated.

TLDR: I’ve started making small lifestyle improvements in sleep, diet, exercise, and planning, and I feel really good about it. Next step is building better study habits.

original_text:
So for years I was a check to check machinist in Texas, making maybe 2800 every 2 weeks after taxes, working 6 12s. With a 1 year old and a 2 year old going through diapers and formula was brutal, wife at home caring for them was a bit strenuous on our finances but better than a daycare financially. I got tired of it, and the stress  was starting to eat into our life as a family..... One day I saw a job listing for Traveling machinist, pay was 32 an hour, plus 700 a week in Per Diem (not taxed, cant be taxed). So the job would move me from place to place 6 -24 months at a time, hourly pay would change, but nothing less than 32, required to work a minimum of 48 hours, and I can choose to work a max of 72 if I please. So I apply, I get the offer, and the day I get the offer my pay rate goes up to 36 an hour. The place I am at is looking at 2-3 years of us contractors being here, which is awesome for me...... now I make 2100 working 4 days a week, and 3300 working 6 days a week with the flexibility of choosing how I want to work and when i want to work.... it was a fucking scary jump to make, i risked what we had in savings to get me up here and last me till i started to get income again, and not knowing how it would work out for me up here...

We are as happy as ever.

All i want to say to yall is, TAKE THAT CHANCE IN LIFE..... it may be scary, it may be very hard with a family... but life isn't worth living without risk involved... take the leap into something scary if the juice is worth the squeeze.
text:
For years I was living paycheck to paycheck as a machinist in Texas, making around $2,800 every two weeks after taxes while working 6 days of 12-hour shifts. With a 1-year-old and a 2-year-old, diapers and formula were expensive, and although my wife staying home to care for them saved daycare costs, it still put a strain on our finances. The stress eventually started affecting our family life.

One day I came across a job posting for a traveling machinist role paying $32/hour plus $700 per week in tax-free per diem. The job involved moving between locations every 6–24 months, with pay never going below $32/hour, a minimum of 48 hours per week, and the option to work up to 72 hours.

I applied, got the offer, and on the same day my pay was increased to $36/hour. The current site I’m at is expected to last 2–3 years for contractors, which works out great for me. Now I make about $2,100 working 4 days a week and up to $3,300 working 6 days a week, with full flexibility over my schedule.

It was a scary leap. I risked our savings to relocate and support us until income started again, not knowing how things would turn out.

But now we’re happier than ever.

All I want to say is: take the chance. It might be scary and difficult, especially with a family, but life isn’t meant to be risk-free. If the reward is worth it, take the leap.

From ages 15-21, I knew nothing except being sporadically ill and moving back and forth from hospital to hospital and doctor to doctor. Then a month later an attack on public transportation causing me to injure my ankle, leading to many months of surgery and recovery. Along with handling the grief of what happened to me. My own family tried to tell me it was my fault. 
So for the rest of that year, I continued to freelance but of course it’s not the kind of money I want. I still live with my toxic family in a shitty cramped apartment. I was miserable last fall because everything went wrong. I applied to 800 jobs between then and now and never get called back. And when I do get called back, the interview is where it ends. I have a feeling they can detect my anxiety. 

I am now almost 25 and haven’t accomplished anything in life and I have no idea how I’m supposed to escape poverty or my toxic family besides joining the military, which seems like a terrible option since I have a clear history of chronic illness and depression on my medical record. - dropped out of school many years ago, applied again at 22, during Covid. 

- was accepted Jan of 2021. But had to take a break due to a traumatic injury and surgery. 

- I am now 24, attending school online. still unfortunately live with the toxic family because of years of health issues, both mentally and physically in which set me back. They don’t think I have a real problem. They think I’m just lazy/stupid and that it’s my fault. 

- never applied for the military due to the history of health problems on my record, including therapy for depression, anxiety, and panic disorder, which would disqualify me.

What should I do? I have no idea how to get out of my situation and I often don’t see hope in it. I feel like years of illness and trauma has beaten me down and now I’m afraid I’m giving up.

I have been diagnosed with severe anxiety/panic disorder and ptsd. I also have ibs, gerd, and possibly another disorder called gastroparesis. From ages 15-21, I knew nothing except being sporadically ill and moving back and forth from hospital to hospital and doctor to doctor. Compounded with my depression and verbally abusive family members, this initially led me to dropping out of college. My only sibling I grew up with was pretty unsympathetic and remained so until this day. Pretty much my whole family, fails to understand how it is to live in fear everyday and refused to help me make the transition to college because they thought I was being lazy and dumb. Because of the years of depression and health issues, my last non freelance job was in 2019. 

However, when I turned 22 (Feb 2020), I made the leap towards finding my way to college. Covid happened, so I studied to brush up on my skills and made it to university by January 2021. Then a month later an attack on public transportation causing me to injure my ankle, leading to many months of surgery and recovery. Along with handling the grief of what happened to me. My own family tried to tell me it was my fault. 
So for the rest of that year, I continued to freelance but of course it’s not the kind of money I want. I still live with my toxic family in a shitty cramped apartment. I was miserable last fall because everything went wrong. I applied to 800 jobs between then and now and never get called back. And when I do get called back, the interview is where it ends. I have a feeling they can detect my anxiety. 

I am now almost 25 and haven’t accomplished anything in life and I have no idea how I’m supposed to escape poverty or my toxic family besides joining the military, which seems like a terrible option since I have a clear history of chronic illness and depression on my medical record. Even at 18, I thought of how bad of an idea it would be because by then I had a bunch of shit on my medical record. I have no idea what to do anymore. 

TLDR:

- grew up working class in a toxic, abusive family household. Family poorly managed money so I had nothing to fall back on. 

- dropped out of school many years ago, applied again at 22, during Covid. 

- was accepted Jan of 2021. But had to take a break due to a traumatic injury and surgery. 

- I am now 24, attending school online. still unfortunately live with the toxic family because of years of health issues, both mentally and physically in which set me back. They don’t think I have a real problem. They think I’m just lazy/stupid and that it’s my fault. 

- never applied for the military due to the history of health problems on my record, including therapy for depression, anxiety, and panic disorder, which would disqualify me. 

- haven’t had a real job since 2019. I always apply to part time jobs but never get called back. I do freelancing but it hardly pays the bills and I’m sick of poverty. I’m sick of sharing a cramped hoarding apartment with a toxic family and barely any privacy. I don’t want to ask the family for help, for obvious reasons. 

What should I do? I have no idea how to get out of my situation and I often don’t see hope in it. I feel like years of illness and trauma has beaten me down and now I’m afraid I’m giving up.

original_text:
I’m going to just jump into it and say I have an Anger Management issue.  For some time I have had this, but took awhile for me to consciously acknowledge it and say it out loud but also because I felt therapy……not that I was too good for it, but because I didn’t think I needed it.  This all changed, especially after the initial lockdown occurred last April.  I’ve become easily triggered by things.  Minor inconveniences, plans changing, etc.  I find that I am complaining about….stupid shit.  I mean I complain about very important shit too, but even then I just keep coming back to it to the point where I feel like it is more than occasional venting. 

I have gotten into dry occasional tiffs with my Wife, and I am holding on to a lot of things that I feel like should be insignificant at this point.  There are other things too, but the point is I am acknowledging the fact that I need some form of Anger Management.  

What I wanted to ask here is if there are things I should be looking for in a therapist.  I have already started looking and there are so many that do specialize in Anger Management that it is a little overwhelming.  I’m sure I will find one that I am comfortable with, but if I don’t can I just move on to another one?  Is that common?
text:
I’m just going to be direct and say it—I have an anger management issue. I’ve struggled with this for a while, but it took me some time to fully admit it out loud. Part of that was also because I didn’t really think I needed therapy—not because I felt above it, but because I didn’t believe my issues were serious enough.

That changed a lot after the initial lockdown last April. I’ve noticed I’ve become much more easily triggered by things like minor inconveniences or changes in plans. I also find myself complaining a lot—sometimes about things that are honestly pretty trivial. Even when I’m dealing with real issues, I tend to keep revisiting them in my mind instead of just venting and moving on.

I’ve had a few minor arguments with my wife, and I realize I’m holding onto things that probably shouldn’t matter as much as they do. There are other examples too, but overall I’ve come to accept that I likely need some form of anger management help.

What I’m trying to figure out now is what to look for in a therapist. I’ve started searching, and there are a lot of therapists who specialize in anger management, which feels a bit overwhelming. I’m confident I’ll find someone I’m comfortable with, but if I don’t, is it normal to switch and try another therapist?

A few days ago, she told me she has feelings for me and asked how I feel about her. I said I was “open-minded” or interested (I hope that didn’t come off wrong). Honestly, I’m not really into long-distance relationships, and I told her that too, but I still said I was open-minded. Since then, I’ve been feeling more uncomfortable because I think what I said may have been misunderstood.

My question is: I’m trying to be more honest than I have been in the past, and what I meant was that I don’t immediately cut people off before meeting them in person. I didn’t mean it as “I have a crush on you” or that I want a relationship right away. I genuinely like her as a friend and enjoy talking to her, but recently she has been sending messages that feel more like they’re meant for people in a relationship.

How can I clarify that I meant something different without hurting her feelings?

I just hope others don’t make the same mistake I did—think carefully before you speak, because words can hurt even when you don’t intend them to. Be more honest upfront than I was.

Thanks in advance :)
